title: Biscuits and gravy
subtitle: Fluffy buttermilk biscuits split and smothered in sausage cream gravy
excerpt: The Southern breakfast standard: tall, flaky buttermilk biscuits split open and covered with a thick, peppery white gravy made from pork sausage drippings, plain flour, and whole milk. Rich, filling, and deeply comforting at any time of day.
sourceNotes: Biscuits and gravy is a staple of Southern and Midwestern American cooking, with roots in the post-Civil War period when pork fat and flour were among the most affordable ingredients available to rural communities. The dish appears in USDA Extension Service publications on Southern cooking from the early twentieth century. The Southern biscuit (a distinct leavened quick-bread) is documented in American domestic cookery from Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544).

content.text: The Southern biscuit is not a British biscuit. It is a small, round, flaky quick-bread, leavened with bicarbonate of soda and baking powder, with cold butter worked through the flour so that the dough stays flaky as it bakes. The key is cold fat handled briefly: overworking the dough develops the gluten and produces a dense, cakey result. The biscuits should be rough and imperfect, with visible layers and a slightly shaggy edge.

The gravy is a simple white sauce built in the sausage fat left in the pan after the meat is cooked. Flour goes in first to make a rough roux, then milk is added in three stages, whisking to prevent lumps. The gravy is seasoned heavily with black pepper, this is the defining flavour note of the dish. It should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon but still pour. The biscuits are split and the gravy goes directly over them.

items.name: Black pepper
items.prepNote: freshly ground, or more to taste

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Whisk the {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour, {{baking-powder}} of baking powder, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} of bicarbonate of soda, and a good pinch of salt together in a large bowl. Add the cold butter cubes and work quickly with your fingertips until the mixture resembles rough breadcrumbs with some larger butter flakes still visible. Make a well in the centre and pour in the {{buttermilk}} of cold buttermilk. Stir with a fork until just combined, the dough will look shaggy. Do not overwork. Turn out onto a lightly floured surface, fold the dough over itself twice, then pat to 2.5 cm thick. Cut into rounds with a 7 cm cutter or a glass, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on a baking tray and bake for 12 to 14 minutes until risen and golden.
Cook the sausage and make the gravy

Cook the crumbled sausage meat in a saucepan over medium heat for 5 to 6 minutes, breaking it up, until browned. Do not drain the fat. Sprinkle the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour over the sausage and stir to coat, cooking for 1 minute. Pour in one third of the {{whole-milk}} of whole milk and whisk vigorously to prevent lumps. Add the remaining milk in two stages, whisking after each addition. Bring to a simmer, stirring, and cook for 4 to 5 minutes until thickened. Season generously with salt and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper, this gravy needs more pepper than seems reasonable.

Split the warm biscuits open with your fingers, not a knife, the split face should be torn and rough so the gravy catches in the layers. Place two biscuit halves per person on a plate and ladle the sausage gravy generously over. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Leave visible butter flakes in the flour mixture; stir the dough only until it just comes together, then handle as little as possible
items.cause: Butter worked too thoroughly into the flour, or dough overworked after the buttermilk was added
items.symptom: Biscuits are dense and cakey rather than flaky

items.fix: Add the first portion of milk off the heat and whisk vigorously before returning to the heat; adding milk in stages prevents lumps
items.cause: Flour not whisked in quickly enough when the milk was added

items.fix: Simmer for a further 3 to 4 minutes, stirring constantly, until the gravy coats the back of a spoon
items.cause: Not reduced long enough after all the milk was added

items.fix: Add black pepper in stages and taste after each addition; the gravy should have an unmistakable pepperiness. Ensure the sausage is properly browned before adding flour
items.cause: Insufficient black pepper, or sausage not browned enough before making the gravy

content.text: Biscuits and gravy started as a thrifty Southern and Midwestern breakfast. Flour, fat, milk, and cheap sausage were what most farm households had on hand. The dish made the most of every scrap. It moved from farm kitchens to diner menus and on to the big breakfast chains. Through all of that it kept the feel of something made at home. Few breakfasts are as filling. That is both the point and the catch.
